content:
4 min read 2 hours ago -- React Native JS LogoWork is moving well on the new social app that I have been working on for nearing a month now. To remind anyone following along, the goal of this app is to recapture what social media can be, to focus on community over algorithms, and to keep the conversation focused on people and facts rather than bots and misinformation. If you are interested in the joining the app once it is released, you can sign up for the waitlist here: Commons App. In this article, I am going to talk a little bit about starting the process of building a mobile app. As I am building a social media platform here, I will be using React Native along with its development platform Expo to test and publish the app. In this article, I wanted to give a little guide of how to get the app setup and how to get it running with expo for those who may be interested in trying this themselves. Since I’m using Expo, the initial setup process is significantly smoother than bare React Native, allowing me to bypass complex native environment configurations (like Xcode or Android Studio) right out of the gate. This is a massive time saver when you just want to get code running and focus on features. For this guide, I also suggest getting vscode installed on your computer (its available on every platform I believe). Initial Setup & Project Creation Before I start, I made sure I had Node.js installed (which handles NPM/Yarn). This is the foundational requirement for any React project. I used npx because it's the recommended way to create a new Expo app without installing global dependencies. To get everything installed and created correctly you can go ahead and run: # 1. Create the project structure using npx npx create-expo-app commons-app# 2. Navigate into the newly created directory cd commons-app # (Optional but recommended) Install a navigation library early on npm install react-native-screens react-native-safe-area-context Running The App This is the best part. Once I run this single command, Expo handles bundling everything and provides me with the necessary QR code or connection details to view the app immediately. You are going to need to download the expo mobile testing app for this but it is free on android and iOS. Here is the expo command to start the app: npx expo start # (Or simply npm start)What happens next: • A Metro Bundler window opens in my terminal. • I scan the generated QR code with the Expo Go app on my physical phone, OR • I press w to simulate running it on a web browser (good for testing components quickly), OR • I run the appropriate platform command (ios or android) if I prefer using an emulator. Now, let’s take a look at some of the code that it generated to get a better idea of what is actually going on when you create a react native app. Understanding The Boilerplate Code By default, Expo sets up a clean basic file structure. For this guide, we are only touching one main file: App.js (or App.tsx if I chose TypeScript). This is where the entire component lifecycle starts. I’m going to refactor App.js to show how modern React Native development looks—using functional components and hooks. import 'react-native'; // Good practice import import { View, Text } from 'react-native'; import React from 'react';export default function App() { return ( Hello World ); } So firstly we need to import ‘react-native’ so the metro bundler knows to include that package. Then, we need to import our view components ‘View’ and ‘Text’. Our view components work a little differently in React Native, there is no
or

, instead every dom element must be imported from the React Native package. Next, we create our App() component, which is just a function that we specify as our default export. From here, the export is then processed by the bundler and specified as our main app screen. Finally, we have our return function that returns the structure of our DOM elements. You can see I just have a ‘Hello World’ screen displayed here but I will get into more complex view components as time goes on. Additionally, any code can be included above the return() statement in the component — this is where you can use the react hooks such as useState and useEffect, which is why I also have the base React package imported, although it is not used in this case. Overview I hope this guide is helpful in getting a basic react native app up and running. Testing with expo is simple so I highly suggest using it over trying to just use bare react native packages, it makes the setup way easier. Once again if you enjoy the articles and are interested in joining the app, consider checking the app out here: Commons App. Also check out my other projects if you are a sports fan: Propicks Web: Propicks Propicks Mobile: Propicks Social
